Mr. Edward Lopez, Past President 
Legislative Committee Chairman 
Central Coast Polygraph 
118 Bel Air Drive 
Scotts Valley, CA 
90566 
Dear Mr. Lopez: 
322·5901 
322-6441 
March 4, 1985 
RE: 
FPPC Advice Letter A85-023 
I apologize for the delay in responding to your letter of 
February 4, 1985. 
The facts as I understand them are as follows: 
The California Association of Polygraph Examiners has a 
pledge program where members of the Association may pledge a 
monetary amount for future contributions to state legislators. 
Because the pledge cards were received late this year, the 
Association's Board of Directors authorized a $1,000 loan to the 
pledge program. 
The $1,000 has been contributed to a state-
elected officeholder's fundraiser. 
The loan will be repaid to 
the Association's general fund by pledges it receives from its 
members. 
You asked if the Association will have to register as a 
committee and file campaign statements. 
Government Code Section 82013 defines committee to mean "any 
person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly: 
(a) receives contributions totaling five hundred dollars ($500) 
or more in a calendar year: ••• " 
As soon as the Association receives $500, it will have to 
register with the Secretary of State's office and file campaign 
statements. 
I have enclosed for your information and use Forms 410, 420, 
450, the 1985 "Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure 
Provisions of the Political Reform Act," "Guide for Candidates 
and Treasurers," and "Recordkeeping Manual." 


Mr. Edward Lopez 
March 4, 1985 
Page 2 
I hope the above information is of assistance. 
Please do not 
hesitate to contact me if you have additional questions.

Edward Lopez, Past President

sections.question: By loaning this money to the pledge program, does the Association have to register with your agency? If so, what registration documents are necessary? And, will a one time registration suffice if in the future we advise our members to make individual donations to legislators as has occurred in the past?
sections.conclusion: As soon as the Association receives $500, it will have to register with the Secretary of State's office and file campaign statements.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the California Association of Polygraph Examiners must register as a committee and file campaign statements after loaning $1,000 to a pledge program that contributes to state legislators' fundraisers, and whether one-time registration would suffice for future individual member donations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under Government Code Section 82013, once the Association receives contributions totaling $500 or more in a calendar year, it must register as a committee with the Secretary of State's office and file campaign statements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request letter (February 4, 1985) and FPPC response letter (March 4, 1985). Advice letter A85-023. Clear question and conclusion sections present in the response portion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: By loaning this money to the pledge program, does the Association have to register with your agency? If so, what registration documents are necessary? And, will a one time registration suffice if in the future we advise our members to make individual donations to legislators as has occurred in the past?

CONCLUSION: As soon as the Association receives $500, it will have to register with the Secretary of State's office and file campaign statements.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry about whether an association's $1,000 loan to a political pledge program triggers committee registration requirements under California's Political Reform Act. FPPC advises that registration is required once the association receives $500 or more in contributions in a calendar year.
